content.text: Hypercare Exit: Why Criteria Matter More Than Calendars

The most common hypercare failure is exiting on a fixed 30-day schedule regardless of service health. Hypercare exists to protect users during the period of highest post-launch risk. Exiting hypercare before the service is stable removes the safety net prematurely. Conversely, maintaining hypercare indefinitely wastes engineering capacity and delays the handoff of ownership to SRE.

Exit criteria translate a calendar target into a set of observable, measurable conditions. When all conditions are met, exit is safe. When any condition fails, the service gets a 7-day remediation window and the gate is re-evaluated — there is no fixed deadline for exit, only a minimum: Day 30.

attrs.alt: Hypercare exit gate — seven criteria with pass/fail outcomes and owners

content.text: The 7-day rolling average eval pass rate must be at or above the SLO threshold of 94% and must have been stable for the last 7 days. A single day of high eval scores that follows several days of degradation does not satisfy this criterion — the trend must show sustained recovery.

Why 7-day stability matters: eval metrics for GenAI services have high day-to-day variance due to query mix differences. A 7-day window smooths out daily noise and confirms that the quality is structurally stable, not just a good day in an otherwise degraded window.

There must be no P0 safety incidents in the last 7 days, and the safety flag rate must be at or below 0.05% of all requests. Safety incidents are the one category that can trigger an extension from Day 30 back to warroom-level coverage, regardless of all other metrics. A service with an elevated safety signal should never exit hypercare.

No P0 or P1 incidents in the last 7 days, and the HTTP 5xx error rate below 0.1%. The incident requirement is stricter than the metric requirement: a service could theoretically have a low aggregate error rate while still having had a P1 incident (for example, a 30-minute outage that has since recovered). Both conditions must hold independently.

All runbooks must have been reviewed by the SRE who is taking primary on-call ownership, not just by the feature team who wrote them. The CI audit must pass. At least one runbook must have been exercised in a drill — reading a runbook is not the same as executing it.

The PagerDuty schedule must show SRE as the primary for the post-hypercare period. The feature team must have been removed from the primary on-call layer. Feature team engineers remain as the escalation path for issues that require code changes, but they are not the first call.

All P0 and P1 incidents that occurred during hypercare must have completed post-incident reviews with their action items tracked in the issue tracker. Open PIR action items indicate unresolved root causes that the SRE inheriting the service will not understand — they must be resolved or explicitly transferred before exit.

A structured review meeting with SRE, feature team, ML Platform, and Support must be held before exit. This meeting is the mechanism for knowledge transfer that cannot be encoded in documentation: edge cases, quirks of the model's behavior under specific query patterns, workarounds currently in place, and known risks that do not yet have mitigation tickets.
The metric-based exit criteria are automated so the 30-day review meeting can focus on qualitative discussion rather than manual metric review:

Exit check failures should be reported to the specific gate owner, not to the whole team. Sending 'eval gate failing' to the SRE lead when the owner is ML Platform creates noise and diffuses accountability.

The transition is not an event — it is a gradient that begins at Day 14 when SRE takes primary on-call and completes at Day 30 when the feature team is fully removed from rotation. The exit meeting formalizes what has already been in progress for two weeks.
SRE takes primary if gates pass
Feature team to business hours only
SRE fully primary, feature on-call only
Re-run gate check weekly until pass

A generated handoff document captures the service's operational profile and is handed to the SRE team at exit. It is generated from structured service metadata rather than written manually to ensure completeness:

# scripts/generate_handoff_doc.py
# Generate the hypercare exit handoff document from service metadata.
import datetime, json

def generate_handoff(service: dict, sre_lead: str,
                     exit_date: datetime.date) -> str:
    return f"""# Hypercare Exit Handoff: {service['name']}
exit_date: {exit_date}
sre_lead: {sre_lead}
feature_team_lead: {service['feature_lead']}

## What SRE Now Owns
- Primary on-call for all P0-P2 incidents
- Prometheus alert triage and escalation
- Runbook maintenance (90-day review cycle)
- Quarterly rollback drill execution

## What Feature Team Retains
- Code changes required for incident resolution
- Escalation path for model-specific issues
- Eval baseline updates after model changes
- 30-day check-in with SRE after hypercare exit

## Active SLOs
{json.dumps(service['slos'], indent=2)}

## Known Risks and Mitigations
{chr(10).join(f'- {r}' for r in service['known_risks'])}

## Escalation Contacts
| Issue type | Contact | Channel |
|------------|---------|--------|
| Safety/PII | Trust & Safety on-call | #trust-safety-oncall |
| Model/eval | ML Platform on-call | #ml-platform-oncall |
| Provider outage | Vendor TAM | email |
"""

# Example
service = {
    'name': 'genai-answer-service',
    'feature_lead': '@alice',
    'slos': [
        {'name': 'availability', 'target': '99.9%'},
        {'name': 'p99_latency', 'target': '<2.5s'},
        {'name': 'eval_pass_rate', 'target': '>=94%'},
    ],
    'known_risks': [
        'Eval scores drop ~2pp when provider upgrades model without notice',
        'Token cost spikes on Mondays due to batch re-indexing job',
    ],
}
doc = generate_handoff(service, '@bob-sre', datetime.date(2026, 7, 31))
print(doc)
The review agenda is templated to ensure all required topics are covered. The live exit gate check at the end of the meeting makes the go/no-go decision visible to all participants:

# 30-Day Review Agenda — genai-answer-service
date: 2026-07-31
attendees: feature-team-lead, sre-lead, ml-platform-lead, support-lead, PM

## 1. Metrics Review (15 min)
- Eval pass rate trend over 30 days
- Safety flag rate trend
- P99 latency and error rate
- Token cost vs. budget

## 2. Incident Review (15 min)
- List all P0/P1/P2 incidents in the hypercare window
- PIR action item status: done / in-progress / blocked
- Any incident patterns that suggest systemic issues

## 3. Runbook and Alerting Review (10 min)
- Were any runbooks used? Did they work as written?
- Alert fatigue: any alerts firing that turned out to be noise?
- Missing alerts: any incidents that were not caught by alerting?

## 4. Handoff Confirmation (10 min)
- SRE confirms primary on-call ownership
- Feature team confirms escalation path is understood
- Support confirms AI-specific ticket routing is working

## 5. Exit Gate Check (10 min)
- Run hypercare_exit_check.py live
- If any gate fails: assign owner and set 7-day remediation window

## 6. Decisions
- [ ] Exit hypercare (all gates pass)
- [ ] Extend hypercare by 7 days (specify gate and owner)
- [ ] Escalate to leadership (systemic issue or repeated P0s)
When one or more gates fail at Day 30, hypercare is extended by 7 days. The extension protocol ensures that extensions are purposeful, not indefinite:
Identify the failing gate and its owner.
The gate owner is responsible for the remediation, not the whole team.
Set a specific remediation action with a due date.
'Improve eval' is not a remediation action. 'Retrain prompt on the identified failure cases in PR #456, due Day 33' is.
Run the automated exit check plus any manual gates that require human sign-off.
Do not drain coverage while a gate is failing, even if the failing gate appears minor.
Two consecutive extensions (Days 30, 37) indicate a systemic issue. Leadership should be aware and may need to reallocate resources.
Remediation plan with owner and due date
Consider Week 2 if new P1 occurred
After a successful exit, the operational model shifts in several concrete ways:

Hypercare exit does not mean the service no longer needs attention. It means the service has demonstrated enough stability that it can be operated under the same model as other production services. If the service later regresses, hypercare-like coverage can always be reinstated.

Hypercare exit is gated on seven criteria: eval pass rate stability above 94% for 7 days, no active safety incidents, no P0/P1 incidents in the last 7 days, completed runbook review by SRE, confirmed PagerDuty handoff, completed PIRs for all incidents, and a 30-day review meeting. The automated exit check script verifies the metric-based criteria; human sign-offs cover the process-based criteria. When any gate fails, hypercare is extended by 7 days with a specific remediation plan — there is no maximum number of extensions, only a minimum of Day 30. After exit, primary on-call ownership transfers fully to SRE, response SLAs loosen to steady-state levels, and the feature team shifts to the escalation path rather than the first call.
